did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception) to classify different types of folliculitis, a skin condition, using a custom dataset of 502 dermatological images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work investigating different deep learning models (AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception) for classifying folliculitis. It details a custom dataset of 502 images, describes the methodology including fine-tuning and image augmentation, and reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) in Table II. Although published in a conference proceedings (INCET), it contains original results and is not a review.

title: Investigating Different Deep learning Models for Classification of Folliculitis
brief_summary: This study investigates the efficacy of seven deep learning Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models—AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception—for classifying eight different types of folliculitis. Due to the lack of existing benchmarks, the authors constructed a custom dataset of 502 dermatological images verified by a dermatologist. The models were fine-tuned and evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a laptop with an Nvidia MX350 GPU. The experimental results demonstrated that GoogLeNet achieved the highest classification accuracy of 41.26%, outperforming the other tested architectures. The authors conclude that while the accuracy is modest, the study provides a promising foundation for automated folliculitis diagnosis, suggesting that larger datasets and hyperparameter tuning could improve future performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies eight specific types of folliculitis using a custom dataset of 502 images. The distribution is: Bacterial folliculitis (31), Boils (71), Eosinophilic folliculitis (102), Gram-negative folliculitis (27), Pityrosporum folliculitis (54), Pseudofolliculitis barbae (108), Pseudomonas folliculitis (56), and Sycosis barbae (53).

all_task_types.target: Eight different types of folliculitis (Bacterial folliculitis, Boils, Eosinophilic folliculitis, Gram-negative folliculitis, Pityrosporum folliculitis, Pseudofolliculitis barbae, Pseudomonas folliculitis, Sycosis barbae)
all_task_types.details: The paper investigates seven deep learning CNN models (AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception) to classify dermatological images into discrete diagnostic categories of folliculitis subtypes. The task is explicitly defined as classification, with performance evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a custom dataset of 502 images.

datasets_used.name: Custom Folliculitis Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of 502 high-resolution dermatological images of different types of folliculitis, collected from various websites across the internet and verified by a dermatologist. The paper states that no existing dataset was available for this specific task, necessitating the creation of this dataset.

use.training: Used for training seven pre-trained CNN models (AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception) with fine-tuning and image augmentation (horizontal reflection, random shifting, random scaling).
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical inference.

Custom Folliculitis Dataset.notes: GoogLeNet achieved the highest classification accuracy of 41.26%. The study notes that while the accuracy may seem modest, it is a promising foundation as no previous benchmarks were available for comparison. Performance can be improved by increasing dataset size, improving image quality, and fine-tuning hyperparameters.

architecture_used.main_model: Seven Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models: AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were used and fine-tuned for the specific task of classifying folliculitis subtypes.
architecture_used.input_size: AlexNet: 227x227x3; ResNet50: 224x224; VGG19: 224x224; Xception: 224x224. Other models' specific input sizes were not explicitly detailed in the text.

architecture_details.AlexNet: 8 levels, 5 convolutional layers with max pooling, followed by 3 fully connected layers. Uses ReLU activation. Won ILSVRC 2012.
architecture_details.DenseNet201: Introduced by Huang et al. (2017). Uses dense connectivity to address vanishing gradient, improve feature propagation, and reduce parameters.
architecture_details.GoogLeNet: 22 layers deep. Incorporates Inception blocks with convolutions of kernel sizes 1x1, 3x3, and 5x5. Top-performing model in ILSVRC 2014.
architecture_details.InceptionV3: 48 layers deep. Uses parallel combinations of different convolutional filters to improve efficiency and accuracy.
architecture_details.ResNet50: Deep residual network by He et al. (2016). Uses residual blocks with skipped connections on 2 or 3 layers employing ReLU and batch normalization to solve vanishing/exploding gradient problems.
architecture_details.VGG19: Uses very small receptive fields. VGG-19 model consists of 19 layers.
architecture_details.Xception: 71 layers deep. Advanced version of Inception using modified depthwise separable convolutions. Surpasses Inception on ImageNet ILSVRC and JFT datasets.

training_details.fine_tuning: The final Fully Connected layer and output layer of all pre-trained CNN networks were deleted and replaced with a new Fully Connected layer.
training_details.augmentation: Horizontal reflection (50% probability), random horizontal and vertical shifting, and random scaling along horizontal and vertical dimensions.
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i5-1135G7 CPU, Nvidia MX350 GPU, 8GB RAM.
training_details.hyperparameters: Not reported (authors suggest future work should fine-tune hyperparameters)

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: New Fully Connected layer replacing the original final layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Image augmentation: Horizontal reflection with 50% probability
Image augmentation: Random horizontal and vertical shifting
Image augmentation: Random scaling along horizontal and vertical dimensions
Fine-tuning: Deletion of the final Fully Connected layer and output layer of pre-trained CNN networks, replaced with a new Fully Connected layer

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with 50% probability to prevent overfitting.
Images were randomly shifted horizontally and vertically by a certain number of pixels.
Images were randomly scaled along the horizontal and vertical dimensions.

conclusions: GoogLeNet achieved the highest classification accuracy of 41.26% among the seven tested deep learning CNN models (AlexNet, DenseNet201, GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, VGG19, and Xception) for classifying eight types of folliculitis.

The study established a baseline for automated folliculitis classification, noting that the obtained accuracy, while modest, is promising given the lack of previous benchmarks or existing datasets for this specific task.
The authors acknowledge that model performance can be improved by increasing the dataset size, improving image quality, and fine-tuning hyperparameters.
The study highlights the potential for deep learning to aid dermatologists in accurately diagnosing folliculitis subtypes, which is critical for effective treatment and avoiding misdiagnosis.

Future research directions include exploring other deep learning techniques to yield higher accuracy rates and potentially developing applications to collect more data from users or collaborate with hospitals.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small custom dataset of only 502 images, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
The highest classification accuracy achieved was modest (41.26% by GoogLeNet), indicating limited diagnostic utility in its current form.
The dataset was constructed by collecting images from various websites across the internet, introducing potential selection bias and lack of controlled clinical provenance.
There is no external validation; the models were evaluated solely on the internal custom dataset without splitting into independent training, validation, and testing sets.
Key hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, optimizer, loss function) and the specific train/validation/test split ratios were not reported.
The study was conducted on consumer-grade hardware (Intel Core i5, Nvidia MX350 GPU), which may not reflect performance in clinical or high-performance computing environments.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare model performances.
The paper acknowledges that image quality and dataset size are critical limitations and suggests future work to improve these factors.
